steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Ehmann and colleagues hypothesized that immature males — unlike females, who stay home — benefit more from peering at immigrant orangutans. Supporting data must show that males directed a higher proportion of peering at immigrants than females did.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Individual 15 directed a higher proportion of peering events at immigrants to its home region than did individual 16, and individual 1 directed a higher proportion of peering events at immigrants to its home region than did individual 6.: Compares two males to each other (15 vs. 16) and two females to each other (1 vs. 6) — never compares males to females. This shows within-sex variation, not the predicted sex difference. ✗
  • Choice B — Individual 16 had the highest total number of peering events observed at 80.: Reports a total count, not a proportion, and makes no comparison between sexes. Irrelevant to the hypothesis. ✗
  • Choice C — Individual 6 and individual 1 directed a lower proportion of peering events at immigrants to their home regions than did individual 15 and individual 16.: Individuals 1 and 6 are females; individuals 15 and 16 are males. This directly shows females < males in proportion of peering at immigrants — exactly what the hypothesis predicts. ✓
  • Choice D — The proportion of peering events directed at immigrants to immature orangutans' home regions ranged from a low of 0.33 to a high of 1.00.: Describes the overall range across all individuals with no breakdown by sex. Does not support the sex-specific hypothesis. ✗
That matches Choice C — Individual 6 and individual 1 directed a lower proportion of peering events at immigrants to their home regions than did individual 15 and individual 16..
